perturbation.original_text: 2.4 The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 The Recipient shall establish and maintain reasonable security measures (including any suitable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'adequate' to 'reasonable' to describe security measures in 2.4 introduces ambiguity. 'Adequate' suggests a standard that ensures full protection, while 'reasonable' implies a less stringent, cost-considered approach. In the event of a security breach, it's unclear what the exact standard of protection is. This in-text contradiction now affects Clause 5.2 as the exact standard of protection is questionable now.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the standard of security measures from 'adequate' to 'reasonable'. This change introduces ambiguity and weakens the level of protection required. Clause 5.2 states that the Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against all losses arising from any breach of the agreement. Because the term 'reasonable' introduces a less stringent requirement compared to 'adequate', it is now unclear what constitutes a breach of the agreement, creating a contradiction regarding the level of security required. This shift directly impacts the enforceability of the indemnification clause, creating a strong, in-text contradiction. The Misaligned Terminology definition applies here.

perturbation.original_text: 4. RESERVATION OF RIGHTS AND ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 4.1 All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreement. 4.2 Except as expressly stated in this Agreement, no party makes any express or implied warranty or representation concerning its Confidential Information, or the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. 4.3 The disclosure of Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party shall not form any offer by, or representation or warranty on the part of, the Disclosing Party to enter into any further agreement. 4.4 The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. RESERVATION OF RIGHTS AND ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 4.1 All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those implicitly stated in this Agreement. 4.2 Except as expressly stated in this Agreement, no party makes any express or implied warranty or representation concerning its Confidential Information, or the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. 4.3 The disclosure of Confidential Information by the Disclosing Party shall not form any offer by, or representation or warranty on the part of, the Disclosing Party to enter into any further agreement. 4.4 The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'expressly stated' to 'implicitly stated' in 4.1 inverts the meaning of obligations. 'Expressly stated' ensures obligations are clear and definite, while 'implicitly stated' introduces ambiguity, suggesting obligations could be inferred or assumed. This creates a direct contradiction with the intended clarity of the agreement, and creates doubt for clause 5.1, since the obligations are not explicit anymore.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the Recipient and to authorise the Recipient to use such Confidential Information for the Purpose.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'expressly stated' to 'implicitly stated' in Section 4.1 fundamentally alters the nature of obligations within the agreement. The original text ensures that obligations are clear and explicitly defined. The changed text introduces ambiguity by suggesting obligations could be inferred. This contradicts the warranty provided in the Contradicted Text: 'Each Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the Recipient and to authorise the Recipient to use such Confidential Information for the Purpose,' as the scope and nature of the information and authorization, tied to those ambiguous obligations, become unclear, leading to a direct conflict and creating uncertainty about the Disclosing Party's rights and responsibilities.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any serious breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the intentional actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "any breach" to "any serious breach" and "actions or omissions" to "intentional actions or omissions" within 5.2, raises the threshold for indemnity. It introduces a contradiction. The original text covers all breaches, but now only serious breaches that are "intentional" trigger indemnity. Since now only those type of breaches will trigger indemnity, it creates doubt on the validity of Clause 8.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Failure to exercise, or any delay in exercising, any right or remedy provided under this Agreement or by law shall not constitute a waiver of that or any other right or remedy, nor shall it preclude or restrict any further exercise of that or any other right or remedy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 5.2 raises the threshold for indemnity by changing "any breach" to "any serious breach" and "actions or omissions" to "intentional actions or omissions." This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that failure to exercise any right or remedy shall not constitute a waiver. By limiting indemnity to only serious and intentional breaches, the changed text effectively waives the right to remedies for other types of breaches, creating a direct conflict. The explanation also states that 'it creates doubt on the validity of Clause 8.1', which is the contradicted text.
